claim: Cu-containing low-density steel exhibits more fine twins and a greater dislocation density, thereby improving its plasticity and yield strength.
evidence_text: Xie et al.'s research [15] on low-density steel with alloyed Cu revealed that Cu-containing steel has more fine twins and a greater dislocation density, thereby improving its plasticity and yield strength.

claim: The ASTM grain size grade of 0Cu is 10.2, while 1Cu and 3Cu exhibit grades of 9.5 and 9.6, respectively, indicating that Cu addition increases the average austenite grain size.
evidence_text: The ASTM grain size grade of 0Cu is 10.2. After the addition of 1 wt% Cu, the ASTM grain size grade was reduced to 9.5. After adding 3 wt% Cu, the ASTM grain size grade of 3Cu was 9.6... The grain size grade of ASTM steels is inversely proportional to the average grain size; thus, the average grain size of the austenite in the Cu-containing sample steel is greater than that in the Cu-free sample steel.

claim: In 1Cu steel, Al and Mo are uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix; Nb and C co-localize at white particles, suggesting NbC carbides, and slight Cu segregation occurs along austenite grain boundaries.
evidence_text: As shown in Fig. 3(a), the Al and Mo are almost uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix of 1Cu. As shown in the area marked by the red box, the aggregation positions of Nb and C correspond to the aggregation positions of white particles... Therefore, the white particles may be NbC-type carbides. The figure also shows slight aggregation of Cu along the grain boundaries at the austenite grain boundaries of 1Cu.

claim: In 3Cu steel, Al is unevenly distributed at some grain boundaries, strong Cu enrichment occurs at grain boundaries, and white particles consist of elemental Cu and possibly remnant NbC carbides.
evidence_text: As shown in Fig. 3(b), the Al content in 3Cu is unevenly distributed at some grain boundaries, and the grain boundaries are strongly enriched with elemental Cu. Therefore, some white particles on the surface and at the grain boundaries of the matrix are Cu, and the remaining particles may be NbC carbides.

claim: Fluctuations in the anodic curve are attributed to the strong destructive effect of Cl− ions on the stability of the passivation state.
evidence_text: the anode curve in the figure shows fluctuations, possibly because the presence of Cl− ions has a strong destructive effect on the stability of the passivation state.

claim: Electrochemical parameters from Tafel extrapolation: 0Cu has Ecorr = -383 ± 6 mV, Ef = -305 ± 4 mV, Eb = -215 ± 5 mV, ΔE = 90 ± 8 mV, Ipass = 1.69 ± 0.06 × 10^{-6} A/cm²; 1Cu has Ecorr = -368 ± 7 mV, Ef = -301 ± 3 mV, Eb = -225 ± 4 mV, ΔE = 76 ± 5 mV, Ipass = 1.45 ± 0.08 × 10^{-6} A/cm²; 3Cu has Ecorr = -368 ± 6 mV, Ef = -306 ± 6 mV, Eb = -245 ± 5 mV, ΔE = 61 ± 6 mV, Ipass = 1.07 ± 0.07 × 10^{-6} A/cm².
evidence_text: The polarization curve fitting parameters of 0Cu, 1Cu and 3Cu steel obtained via the Tafel extrapolation method, i.e., the corrosion potential (Ecorr), highest activation potential (Ef), pitting potential (Eb), passivation interval (ΔE) and passivation current density (Ipass), are shown in Table 2.

claim: In the low-frequency region of the Bode diagram, the impedance modulus decreases with increasing frequency; 3Cu exhibits the lowest impedance modulus, consistent with the Nyquist results.
evidence_text: In the low-frequency region of the Bode diagram, the impedance resistance modulus decreases with increasing frequency... 3Cu has the lowest impedance modulus, which is consistent with the results obtained from the previous Nyquist diagram.

claim: EIS data were fitted using the equivalent circuit in Fig. 6(a); Rs values are similar (0Cu: 10.12, 1Cu: 8.329, 3Cu: 8.521 Ω·cm²); CPE values are 1.1697×10^{-4}, 5.8542×10^{-4}, 5.246×10^{-4} Ω^{-1}cm^{-2}·S^{-n} with n=0.81, 0.80, 0.74; Rct decreases from 5796 ± 11 Ω·cm² (0Cu) to 2739 ± 9 (1Cu) to 2062 ± 12 (3Cu), indicating reduced corrosion resistance with Cu.
evidence_text: The equivalent circuit diagram in Fig. 6(a) was selected to fit and analyse the EIS data... The fitted relevant parameters are shown in Table 3.

claim: From Mott-Schottky analysis, the passive film donor density N_D increases with Cu: 0.806×10^{18} cm^{-3} (0Cu), 1.367×10^{18} cm^{-3} (1Cu), 1.446×10^{18} cm^{-3} (3Cu); acceptor density N_A also increases: 2.615×10^{18} cm^{-3} (0Cu), 3.401×10^{18} cm^{-3} (1Cu), 6.066×10^{18} cm^{-3} (3Cu).
evidence_text: Table 4 The donor (N_D) and acceptor (N_A) densities of the passive films of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C-xCu.

claim: As Cu content increases from 0 to 3 wt%, both donor density (ND) and acceptor density (NA) in the passivation film increase, with 0Cu showing the smallest ND and 3Cu the largest.
evidence_text: As the Cu content increased, the values of ND and NA increased accordingly. ... The data show that the ND value of 0Cu is the smallest and that the ND value of 3Cu is the largest.

claim: According to the point defect model, higher donor density ND in 3Cu steel reflects greater charge carrier density, leading to more defects and more intense electrochemical reactions, making 3Cu the most sensitive to pitting nucleation and Cl− ion adsorption.
evidence_text: According to the point defect model (PDM), the adsorption of Cl− ions promotes the nucleation and growth of corrosion pits [52]. Therefore, N reflects the charge density after the formation of the space charge layer and can be used as an indicator to evaluate the nucleation ability of corrosion pits. The data show that the ND value of 0Cu is the smallest and that the ND value of 3Cu is the largest. Therefore, 3Cu has the greatest sensitivity to pitting nucleation and the adsorption of Cl− ions.

claim: Donor and acceptor densities were calculated using Mott-Schottky equations with dielectric constant of passivation film ε=15.6, vacuum permittivity ε0=8.854×10^−12 F/m, electron charge e=1.602×10^−19 C, Boltzmann constant k=1.38×10^−23 J/K, and test temperature T=300 K.
evidence_text: ε represents the dielectric constant of the passivation film (ε = 15.6), and ε0 represents the vacuum dielectric constant (8.854 × 10^−12 F m^−1). ... the electron charge e is 1.602 × 10^−19 C; ... K is 1.38 × 10^−23 J K^−1, which is the Boltzmann constant; and the test temperature is 300 K and is represented by T.

claim: After 1 day of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl, the 0Cu steel exhibited slight pitting corrosion, while the 1Cu and 3Cu steels showed more severe pitting with pronounced corrosion traces; local grain boundaries appeared on the surface of the corroded 3Cu sample.
evidence_text: After 1 day of immersion, slight pitting corrosion occurred in the 0Cu steel, and slight corrosion traces were evident around the pits. The pitting corrosion on the surfaces of the 1Cu steel and 3Cu steel was more severe, and the corrosion traces around the pits were more pronounced. Moreover, local grain boundaries appeared on the surface of the corroded 3Cu sample.

claim: During hot rolling and cooling, metastable phase decomposition leads to redistribution of C and Al in the γ phase, forming solute-poor and solute-rich phases; the solute-rich phase transforms to (Fe,Mn)3Al L12 phase via short-range ordering, and C atoms order to form intragranular κ-carbide.
evidence_text: During hot rolling and cooling, the sample steel undergoes metastable phase decomposition, resulting in the redistribution of C and Al in the γ phase and the formation of a solute-poor phase and solute-rich phase. During further cooling, the solute-rich phase is transformed into the L12 phase, which consists of (Fe,Mn)3Al, via a short-range ordering reaction, whereas the C atoms in the L12 phase continue to form an ordered structure, and an intragranular κ-carbide is formed

claim: Electron diffraction pattern calibration revealed that the carbides in all three steels are MC-type carbides, specifically NbC, as confirmed by EDS point and surface scanning which showed enrichment of Nb, C, W, and Mo, and absence of Fe, Cr, and Al.
evidence_text: The results show that the carbides are MC carbides. ... the carbides contain a large amount of elemental Nb, followed by C. The surface scanning results revealed that almost no Fe, Cr or Al was present in the carbides, whereas the carbides were enriched with elemental C, W, Nb and Mo. ... The electron diffraction patterns in Figs. 11(e) and 12(e) and 13(e) confirmed that the carbide was NbC, which corresponds to the element scanning results of EPMA (Fig. 3).

claim: High-resolution TEM images show fracture phenomena in regular moiré fringes near carbides, attributed to edge dislocations; these dislocations act as anodic sites with high strain energy, reducing corrosion resistance and promoting localized pitting.
evidence_text: The fracture phenomenon (the upper left images in Figs. 11(f)–12(f) and 13(f) are marked with T) is observed in the regular moiré fringes, which can be attributed to edge dislocations [58]. ... the appearance of dislocations deteriorates the corrosion resistance because the dislocation core associated with high strain energy is an anode site relative to the matrix. These regions with dislocations are more prone to dissolution, thereby causing localized pitting

claim: With increasing Cu content from 0Cu to 3Cu, the relative content of Al2O3 in the surface oxide decreased from 38.2% to 16.3%, while MnO and MnO2 increased significantly; Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃, and FeOOH decreased.

The significantly lower Gibbs free energy of Al2O3 (−1582.4 kJ/mol) compared to Fe2O3 (−742.0 kJ/mol), Fe3O4 (−1015.5 kJ/mol), Mn2O3 (−881.1 kJ/mol), and Mn3O4 (−1283.2 kJ/mol) explains why Al is preferentially oxidized in Fe–30Mn–8Al–C steel.
evidence_text: The significant difference in the Gibbs free energy of different oxides at room temperature (Al2O3 is −1582.4 kJ/mol, Fe2O3 is −742.0 kJ/mol, Fe3O4 is −1015.5 kJ/mol, Mn2O3 is −881.1 kJ/mol, and Mn3O4 is −1283.2 kJ/mol) may explain why Al is easily preferentially oxidized.

claim: In the XPS spectra of the three steels after 5-day immersion, the Fe 2p peaks correspond to Fe3O4, Fe2O3, and FeOOH; Mn 2p peaks correspond to MnO and MnO2; Al 2p peak corresponds only to Al2O3; and Cu 2p peaks correspond to CuO and Cu2O.
evidence_text: As shown in Fig. 15(a1–c1), the XPS spectra of Fe in the three steels feature three peaks corresponding to Fe3O4, Fe2O3 and FeOOH. The XPS spectrum of Mn is divided into two peaks corresponding to MnO and MnO2. The XPS spectrum of Al only contains a peak for Al2O3. The XPS spectrum of Cu is divided into two peaks corresponding to CuO and Cu2O.

claim: The relative contents of surface oxides were calculated using the formula C_n = (I_n/S_n) / (∑ I_i/S_i) with sensitivity factors of Fe 14.353, Mn 36, Al 0.56, and Cu 26.513.
evidence_text: The oxide content ratios of the three steels can be calculated via the following formulas to determine the differences in oxide content among different states ... In this work, the sensitivity factors of Fe, Mn, Al and Cu are 14.353, 36, 0.56 and 26.513, respectively.

claim: In 0Cu, the proportion of Mn oxide is 47%; in 1Cu, the proportion of Mn oxide is 57.2%; and in 3Cu, the proportion of Mn oxide is 77%.
evidence_text: In 0Cu, the proportion of Mn oxide is 47%; in 1Cu, the proportion of Mn oxide is 57.2%; and in 3Cu, the proportion of Mn oxide is 77%.

claim: MnO and MnO2 in the oxide film can promote the rate of electron transfer and thereby accelerate the corrosion rate of low-density steel.
evidence_text: The MnO and MnO2 in the oxide film can promote the rate of electron transfer and then accelerate the corrosion rate of low-density steel [68,69].

claim: In 3Cu, enrichment of Cu at grain boundaries and formation of a synergistic corrosion mode with carbides cause the corrosion morphology on the matrix surface to further deteriorate and produce more Mn oxides.
evidence_text: In 3Cu, owing to the enrichment of Cu at the grain boundaries and the formation of a synergistic corrosion mode with carbides, the corrosion morphology on the matrix surface further deteriorated, and more Mn oxides are produced.

claim: After potentiostatic polarization at −0.3 V (vs. Ag–AgCl) in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 7200 s, the stable passivation films on 0Cu and 3Cu contain Fe and Al oxides, which together form an oxide layer to resist Cl− erosion.
evidence_text: The XPS measurement shown in Fig. 17 is the spectral analysis of a stable passivation film obtained by applying a potentiostatic polarization of −0.3 V (vs. Ag–AgCl) in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 7200 s. ... The figure shows that the XPS spectra of 0Cu and 3Cu contain Fe and Al oxides, which together form an oxide layer to resist the erosion of Cl−.

claim: In simulated marine atmosphere, Al atoms preferentially dissolve and oxidize to Al2O3 because the standard electrode potential of Al is lower than those of Fe (-0.447 V vs. SHE) and Mn (-1.179 V vs. SHE).
evidence_text: The standard electrode potential of Al is lower than those of Fe (− 0.447 V vs. SHE) and Mn (− 1.179 V vs. SH) in the simulated marine atmosphere; therefore, Al atoms preferentially dissolve to form Al3+ and then oxidize to Al2O3

claim: The Al2O3 content in the corrosion product is greatest in 0Cu at 38.2%, followed by 1Cu and 3Cu (Fig. 16).
evidence_text: the content of Al2O3 was greatest in 0Cu at 38.2 %, followed by 1Cu and 3Cu (Fig. 16).

claim: As Cu content increases from 0% to 3%, the corrosion mechanism shifts from dislocation-induced corrosion near carbides to a synergistic corrosion effect caused by Cu enrichment at grain boundaries and carbide–matrix potential differences, leading to major decline in corrosion performance.
evidence_text: As the Cu content increases from 0 % to 3 %, the corrosion mechanism shifts from dislocation-induced corrosion near carbides to a synergistic corrosion effect caused by Cu enrichment at the grain boundaries and carbides-matrix potential differences, leading to a major decline in steel corrosion performance.
